coredata.dc:title: Highly sensitive photoelectrochemical sensing of bisphenol A based on zinc phthalocyanine/TiO2 nanorod arrays
coredata.dc:creator: Fan, Zhenzhen
Fan, Lifang
Shuang, Shaomin
Dong, Chuan
coredata.dc:description: A facile and sensitive photoelectrochemical (PEC) sensing platform based on zinc phthalocyanine (ZnPc) sensitized TiO2 nanorodarrays (NRAs) was successfully fabricated for detection of bisphenol A (BPA). The vertically aligned TiO2 NRAs with one-dimensional (1D) nanostructure was synthesized in-situ on fluorine-doped tin oxide-coated glass (FTO) substrate by simple one-step hydrothermal method, and then the TiO2 NRAs/FTO surface was covered with ZnPc. 1D nanostructure of TiO2 NRAs could provide not only more spaces for the load of ZnPc, but direct pathways to reduce the recombination of electron-hole pairs, improving the photocatalytic efficiency. The combination of ZnPc with TiO2 NRAs extended effectively the absorption of TiO2 to visible region and improved greatly the charge transfer ability of TiO2 NRAs. With the addition of BPA, BPA was oxidized by photogenerated holes during PEC reaction so that the photocurrent of the PEC sensing platform increased. BPA could be quantified by measuring the photocurrent change. The developed PEC sensor exhibited outstanding analytical performance with high sensitivity, excellent stability and good reproducibility. A wide linear work range from 0.047 to 52.1 μM was obtained with a low detection limit of 8.6 nM. Meanwhile, the sensor was also used to evaluate the level of BPA in plastic products and food.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Photoelectrochemical sensor
Zinc phthalocyanine
TiO2 nanorod arrays
Bisphenol A

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Characterization of TiO2 NRAs/FTO and ZnPc/TiO2 NRAs/FTO
Highly sensitive PEC sensing for BPA

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: National Natural Science Foundation of China

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency-matched-string: National Natural Science Foundation of China
xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: National Natural Science Foundation of China

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency-matched-string: Natural Science Foundation of Shanxi Province, China

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work was financially supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (NSFC, Nos. 21707082 , 21575084 and 21475080 ), the Natural Science Foundation of Shanxi Province, China (No. 201601D202008 ).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Plane-view FE-SEM images of (A) TiO2 NRAs/FTO and (B) ZnPc/TiO2 NRAs/FTO, the inset is the cross-sectional view of TiO2 NRAs/FTO. (C) The EDS spectrum of ZnPc/TiO2 NRAs/FTO and the inset is the elemental mappings of Ti, O, N and Zn, respectively. (D) N2 adsorption-desorption isotherm of the TiO2 NRAs.
XPS spectra of (A) Ti2p, (B) O1s, (C) Zn2p, (D) N1s for the sample ZnPc/TiO2 NRAs/FTO.

(A) Nyquist diagrams and (B) CV of (a) bare FTO, (b) TiO2 NRAs/FTO, (c) ZnPc/TiO2 NRAs/FTO in 5 mM [Fe(CN)6]3−/4− solution containing 0.1 M KCl. The scan rate of CV was 100 mV s−1. Inset: (1) Randles equivalent circuit, (2) Nyquist diagram of bare FTO.

(A) Photocurrent density of (a) TiO2 NRAs/FTO and (b) ZnPc/TiO2 NRAs/FTO in 0.1 M Na2SO4 at a applied potential of 0.0 V under 420 nm visible irradiation. (B) The dependence of photocurrent density on ZnPc concentrations for vacuum dry depositing 1.0 mL ZnPc on the TiO2 NRAs/FTO, from a to e: 0.1, 0.3, 0.5, 0.8, 1.0 mM, respectively.

(A) Photocurrent density responses of the prepared PEC sensor in 0.1 M Na2SO4 with successive addition of different concentrations of BPA (0.047, 0.071, 0.14, 0.26, 0.69, 1.4, 2.59, 4.50, 6.88, 9.26, 11.6, 18.7, 37.8, 52.1 µM, respectively) at applied potential of 0 V under 420 nm visible irradiation and (B) photocurrent change against BPA concentrations. Inset: the linear analytical curve.
(A) The stability of the PEC sensor in 0.1 M Na2SO4 (B) Selectivity of the prepared PEC sensor for BPA detection.
Schematic representation of the construction of PEC sensor and the mechanism for the PEC detection of BPA.
Comparison of proposed sensor for determination of BPA with different methods.
Determination of BPA in real samples.

head.ce:title: Highly sensitive photoelectrochemical sensing of bisphenol A based on zinc phthalocyanine/TiO2 nanorod arrays

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Institute of Environmental Science, Shanxi University, Taiyuan 030006, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Institute of Environmental Science, Shanxi University

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Shanxi University, Taiyuan 030006, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Shanxi University

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: A facile and sensitive photoelectrochemical (PEC) sensing platform based on zinc phthalocyanine (ZnPc) sensitized TiO2 nanorodarrays (NRAs) was successfully fabricated for detection of bisphenol A (BPA). The vertically aligned TiO2 NRAs with one-dimensional (1D) nanostructure was synthesized in-situ on fluorine-doped tin oxide-coated glass (FTO) substrate by simple one-step hydrothermal method, and then the TiO2 NRAs/FTO surface was covered with ZnPc. 1D nanostructure of TiO2 NRAs could provide not only more spaces for the load of ZnPc, but direct pathways to reduce the recombination of electron-hole pairs, improving the photocatalytic efficiency. The combination of ZnPc with TiO2 NRAs extended effectively the absorption of TiO2 to visible region and improved greatly the charge transfer ability of TiO2 NRAs. With the addition of BPA, BPA was oxidized by photogenerated holes during PEC reaction so that the photocurrent of the PEC sensing platform increased. BPA could be quantified by measuring the photocurrent change. The developed PEC sensor exhibited outstanding analytical performance with high sensitivity, excellent stability and good reproducibility. A wide linear work range from 0.047 to 52.1 μM was obtained with a low detection limit of 8.6 nM. Meanwhile, the sensor was also used to evaluate the level of BPA in plastic products and food.

ce:section.ce:para: Bisphenol A (BPA, 2, 2´-bis(4-hydroxyphenyl)propane), a typical environmental endocrine disruptor [1,2], is commonly utilized as a raw material for the production of polycarbonate (PC) and epoxy resin, which widely exists in numerous plastic products, such as the inner side of protective polymer films used in the food industry, milk bottom, beverage cans, sealants and so on [3,4]. It is not surprising that BPA can eventually be released from the manufacture process and the unintentional migration into the environment and even into food [5,6]. Thus, living beings may inevitably ingest trace amounts of BPA. Unfortunately, the current studies clearly show that BPA possesses hormonal activity, which can replace endogenous estrogen to bind estrogen receptors, and then lead to the endocrine system disorder [2]. Moreover, the low level of BPA exposure is always associated with behavioral disturbance, neurotoxicity, reproductive toxicity and even cancer [7,8]. These potential risks become a serious threat to human being and other organisms. Therefore, the establishment of a simple and effective analytical method for BPA determination is desired.

Numerous analytical methods for BPA have been involved, such as fluorescence spectrophotometry [9], high performance liquid chromatography (HPLC) [10], gas chromatography-mass spectrometry (GC-MS) [11], liquid chromatography-mass spectrometry (LC-MS) [12], enzyme-linked immunosorbent assay (ELISA) [13] and electrochemical methods [14,15]. Among these methods, some require professional operators, expensive equipment, and some require labourious process or severe test conditions. Electrochemical method attracts enormous attention due to its fast response, high sensitivity, real-time detection, simplicity and miniaturization. A variety of electrochemical sensors have been proposed for the determination of BPA based on electrochemical oxidation. However, electrochemical oxidation of BPA can be performed only at a relatively high potential, such as 0.5 V in the SCE scale [16,17]. Moreover, some nonconductive polymeric films formed during oxidation are usually adsorbed easily on the electrode surface, which would inevitably passivate the electrode and affect the sensitivity of BPA detection. So, it is necessary to develop a facile and sensitive method for quantification of BPA. Photoelectrochemical (PEC) method appears to be a promising candidate since it possesses the advantages of both optical and electrochemical techniques. BPA can be easily oxidized at relative low potential during PEC process. Meanwhile, it is appropriate for detection of BPA by PEC method to avoid the passivation of the electrode. Especially, PEC method is an ultrasensitive analytical technique owing to adopting two different kinds of energy as excitation source and detection signal. So the PEC technique is an ideal choice to determine the low level of BPA [18]. Here, what we should note is that selecting an appropriate photoactive material to construct the PEC sensing platform is very critical to achieve high performance for BPA detection.

So far, various photoactive semiconductors such as TiO2 [19], ZnO [20], CdS [21] and CdSe [22] with high photocatalytic activity have been employed to establish PEC sensors. Among these materials, TiO2 has always received more attention because of its perfect photocatalytic activity, photochemical stability, non-toxicity and non-corrosivity [23]. Nevertheless, the intrinsic defects of TiO2, including the wide band gap (only excited by ultraviolet light) and the fast recombination of the photogenerated electron-hole pairs, strongly limit its widespread applications. Thus, in order to enhance the visible-light harvesting ability and the photocatalytic property of TiO2, various attempts have been undertaken, such as metal and non-metal doping [24], dye sensitization [25] or coupling with semiconductor [26]. A practical method is adopted by dye photosensitization TiO2 to enhance the absorption range of TiO2. Metallophthalocyanine complexes (MPc) have aroused widespread concern given its unique thermal and chemical stability, visible light photosensitivity, additional catalytic properties and low price. Through combining the TiO2 and MPc, the absorption of TiO2 can be extended successfully to visible region. Meanwhile, MPc-TiO2 composite can lessen the charge recombination and improve the photocatalytic performance. Among all MPc, the zinc phthalocyanine dye (ZnPc) has a longer excited state lifetime, which is beneficial to the transfer of electrons from the excited state of the dye molecules to TiO2 conduction band [27]. Furthermore, one-dimensional (1D) TiO2 nanostructures can provide direct electrical pathways and speed up electron transportation to reduce the electron-hole recombination. Especially, the rutile TiO2 nanorod arrays (NRAs) with one-dimensional (1D) nanostructures presents excellent electrical transport performance [28].

Herein, a simple and novel PEC sensing platform based on ZnPc sensitized TiO2 NRAs was constructed for detection of BPA. 1D TiO2 NRAs in-situ erectly grown on fluorine-doped tin oxide-coated glass (FTO) was prepared via a rapid and simple one-step hydrothermal. Next, the TiO2 NRAs/FTO surface was covered with ZnPc, using a vacuum drying method, which successfully extended the light absorption of TiO2 to the visible range. The prepared ZnPc/TiO2 NRAs/FTO as a PEC sensing platform was used to detect BPA by measuring the change of photocurrent. The PEC sensor exhibited good analytical performance with a wide linear operating range, high sensitivity, stability and selectivity. Some factors on analytical performance of the PEC sensor were investigated in detailed. In addition, the PEC sensor was applied to evaluate BPA content in actual environmental and food samples such as PC baby bottle, PC bottle, polyvinyl chloride (PVC) food package and milk.

BPA, Tetrabutyltitanate (TBT) and sodiumsulfate (Na2SO4) were purchased from aladdin (LosAngeles, USA). ZnPc was provided by TCI (Shanghai, China). Other reagents were from Beijing Chemical Reagents Company (Beijing, China). All reagents were analytical reagent grade and were used without further purification. All aqueous solutions were prepared with deionized water (DI) (≥ 18.25 MΩ cm) from a Milli-Q Plus system (Millipore, Bedford, MA, USA). FTO (15 × 50 × 2 mm) was purchased from Sheet Glass Nippon, Japan.

The field-emission scanning electron microscope (FE-SEM) was obtained with a JSM-7001F (JEOL, Japan) including an energy dispersive X-ray spectroscopy (EDS) from Bruker (Bremen, Germany). Emmett and Teller (BET) method using the nitrogen adsorption/desorption (Micromeritics ASAP 2460, USA) isotherms. X-ray diffraction (XRD) measurement was performed on a Bruker D8 Venture diffractometer using the Cu Kα as the irradiation (k = 0.15405 nm) at a 2θ scan range of 20–80°. Fourier transform infrared spectra (FTIR) were recorded on a Bruker Tensor II FTIR spectrometer (Bremen, Germany). X-ray photoelectron spectra (XPS) were acquired on an AXIS ULTRA DLD X-ray photoelectron spectrometer (Kratos, Tokyo, Japan) with Al Kα radiation. All PEC experiments were carried out at room temperature with a CHI660C electrochemical workstation (Chenhua Instruments Corp, Shanghai, China) based on a conventional three-electrode system composed of the as-prepared film coated on FTO as working electrode, a platinum plate as counter electrode, and a saturated calomel electrode as reference electrode. The geometrical area of the working electrode was controlled to be 3.0 × 1.5 cm2 with insulating glue.

According to the previous report [29], the single crystal rutile TiO2 NRAs was synthesized on transparent conductive FTO substrates by hydrothermal method. Briefly, FTO glass substrates were cleaned thoroughly by sonication inacetone, anhydrous ethanol and DI for about 15 min, respectively. After drying, the FTO substrates were put into a Teflon-lined stainless steel autoclave at an angle against the wall with the conductive side facing up. 12.5 mL DI containing 2.5 mL saturated aqueous NaCl solution and 15 mL hydrochloric acid (HCl, 36.0–38.0% by weight) were mixed together and stirred for 5 min at ambient condition to achieve a homogeneous solution. Then 400 μL of TBT as titanium precursor was added, followed by another stir until the TBT was completely dissolved. The transparent solution was transferred to a 50 mL Teflon-lined stainless steel autoclave with the FTO substrate. The reactor was transferred into an oven with a temperature at 180 °C maintained for 6 h. While waiting for hydrothermal synthesis to finish and the reactor to cool down to room temperature, the FTO substrate grown by TiO2 NRAs was taken out, carefully washed by DI, and dried using N2 gas flow. Finally, the resultant TiO2 films were suffered from an annealing at 450 °C for 2 h under air condition.

1.0 mL 0.5 mM ZnPc dissolved in N, N-dimethylformamide (DMF) was covered on the TiO2 NRAs/FTO surface, and placed in a vacuum oven at 60 °C until the ZnPc was completely deposited. ZnPc/TiO2 NRAs/FTO was successfully prepared.

All PEC measurements were carried out in 0.1 M Na2SO4 as the supporting electrolyte solution. A 300 W xenon lamp from Perfect Light (Beijing, China) with a single wavelength of 420 nm was used as visible irradiation source. Photocurrent was measured by i-t technique at the constant potential of 0.0 V and the distance between the working electrode and the visible light was 30 cm and the illumination intensity of 50 mW cm−2. Linear sweep voltammetry (LSV) was carried out both in the dark and under visible light from − 0.6 to 1.2 V. Electrochemical impedance spectra (EIS) was measured in the frequency range from 10−1 to 105 Hz under amplitude of 2 mV at open circuit voltage. Cyclic voltammetry (CV) was recorded in the potential from − 0.6 to 1.2 V at 0.1 V s−1. EIS and CV were performed in 5 mM of [Fe(CN)6]3−/4− solution containing 0.1 M KCl. All the experiments were carried out at room temperature in N2 atmosphere.

PC baby bottle, PC bottle, PVC food package and fresh milk were purchased from a local supermarkets (Taiyuan, China). All plastic products were treated according to the previously reported procedure [30]. These samples were cut into small pieces by scissors and washed with DI. Then 2.0 g plastic pieces were completely dissolved into 40 mL dichloromethane, and added into 100 mL methanol to precipitate the polymer material. After that, the mixture was centrifugalized. Supernatant liquid was completely evaporated under rotary evaporation, and then 1.0 mL of ethanol was added to dissolve the residue. The sample solution was filtrated with 0.45 µm filter film, and the filtrate was collected. Finally, it was diluted to 20.0 mL with 0.1 M Na2SO4 and used for BPA analysis.

A mixture solution, 5 mL milk containing 10 mL ethanol, treated with sonicate for 15 min, then centrifuged at 10,000 rpm for 15 min in order to remove the possibly existed polymer particles. The supernatant was filtrated through 0.45 µm of filter membrane. The filtrate was collected and set the volume to 20 mL with 0.1 M Na2SO4.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Characterization of TiO2 NRAs/FTO and ZnPc/TiO2 NRAs/FTO
ce:section.ce:para: The FE-SEM image in Fig. 1A showed the plane-view morphology and the nanostructure of the as-prepared TiO2 NRAs, and the cross-sectional view of TiO2 NRAs was shown in the inset. It was observed that the aligned 1D nanostructure TiO2 NRAs possessing tetragonal cross section were distributed on the FTO substrate. The width of nanorods was in the range of 80–200 nm with the length of 2.5 µm. The surface structure of the TiO2 NRAs was analyzed by nitrogen sorption isotherm techniques. Fig. 1D showed the nitrogen adsorption-desorption isotherms of the sample TiO2 NRAs. The BET specific surface area of the prepared TiO2 NRAs was calculated as 10.746 m2 g−1. The large surface area of 1D nanostructure was beneficial to the increase of the amount of ZnPc adsorption. Particularly, 1D nanostructure could provide direct pathways to reduce the recombination of electron-hole pairs generated under illumination [28]. After loading ZnPc, the morphology of TiO2 NRAs was not obvious change, seen from Fig. 1B. But EDS analysis (Fig. 1C) verified that the elements including Ti, O, C and Zn were present in ZnPc/TiO2 NRAs/FTO, accounted for 37.71%, 26.46%, 39.71% and 5.16%, respectively. Furthermore, the elemental mapping (the inset of Fig. 1C) demonstrated that the Zn element was really dispersed on TiO2 NRAs/FTO surface, further confirmed the successful loading of ZnPc on the TiO2 NRAs. Then XRD patterns proved that the synthetic TiO2 NRAs were rutile and the crystal structure of TiO2 NRAs did not change by the load of ZnPc, as shown in Fig. S1 in the Supporting information (SI).

An XPS study was used to analyze the composition and chemical state of the ZnPc/TiO2 NRAs in order to further confirm that ZnPc was loaded successfully. Fig. 2A showed the splitting peaks of Ti2p, the peak positioned at 464.2 eV corresponded to the Ti2p1/2 and another one located at 458.5 eV was assigned to Ti2p3/2. The energy difference between Ti2p1/2 and Ti2p3/2 was 5.7 eV, indicating a normal state of Ti4+ in the ZnPc/TiO2 NRAs composite [31]. In Fig. 2B, the binding energies of O1s were presented, which had two different binding energy positions. The peaks located at 529.9 eV and 532.4 eV, respectively. It included crystal lattice oxygen (OTi-O), surface hydroxyl groups (OOH) and adsorbed water with increasing binding energy. The high resolution XPS spectrum of Zn2p (Fig. 2C) suggested that the two peaks located at 1020.8 eV and 1043.8 eV corresponded to Zn2p3/2 and Zn2p1/2, respectively. And the splitting between these two peaks was 23.0 eV, clearly demonstrating that zinc species was in the formal Zn2+ valence state [32]. The N1s XPS spectrum was shown in Fig. 2D, the lower binding energy component at 397.8 eV was attributed to the pyridyl N, the peak observed at 399.6 eV was pyridyl N associated with metal Zn. These results confirmed that ZnPc and TiO2 NRAs were successfully combined. And FTIR was also revealed that ZnPc was coated on the surface of TiO2 NRAs shown in SI Fig. S2.

In order to characterize the different process of the PEC sensor preparation, EIS as an effective way to investigate the charge transfer resistance was carried out and shown in Fig. 3A. A Randles equivalent circuit was applied to fit all the EIS results shown in the inset (1) of Fig. 3A. The circuit included the solution resistance (R s), the double layer capacitance of electrode and electrolyte (C), Warburg impedance (Z w), and electron transfer resistance (R et). The impedance spectra of different electrodes was conducted in 5 mM [Fe(CN)6]3-/4- solution containing 0.1 M KCl. EIS of the bare FTO exhibited an extremely small diameter semicircle with the charge transfer resistance value of 12.56 Ω (inset (2)), indicating its excellent electrochemical conductivity After growing TiO2 NRAs on the surface of FTO, the charge transfer resistance value significantly increased to 379.3 Ω (curve b). This reason is that TiO2 semiconductor with the poor conductivity hindered the electron transfer between the electrochemical interface and the probe, [Fe(CN)6]3-/4-. While ZnPc was anchored the TiO2 NRAs/FTO surface, the charge transfer resistance value of the ZnPc/TiO2 NRAs/FTO was 140.2 Ω (curve c), suggesting that ZnPc modified on the TiO2 NRAs/FTO surface can improve the conductivity of the composite. This could be owing to the fact that the good conductivity of ZnPc and the synergistic effect between ZnPc and TiO2 NRAs increased electron transfer ability.

Subsequently, these results were further identified by CV technique in the presence of [Fe(CN)6]3−/4− redox couple. As shown in Fig. 3B, there was a quasi-reversible behavior at the bare FTO (curve a) with a high current density and the peak separation (ΔE p) of about 0.62 V. After modifying TiO2 NRAs on FTO (curve b), the current density decreased and the ΔE p remarkably increased to 0.90 V. This could be ascribed to the fact that the poor conductivity of TiO2 NRAs decreased the electron transfer ability. However, when ZnPc was deposited on TiO2 NRAs/FTO (curve c), the current density obviously increased and the ΔE p decreased again to 0.75 V, suggesting the good conductivity of ZnPc and the synergistic effect of the composite nanomaterial increased the electron transfer ability of the electrode interface. These characterization results were in good agreement with that of EIS.

The PEC response of TiO2 NRAs/FTO and ZnPc/TiO2 NRAs/FTO were shown in Fig. 4A, the TiO2 NRAs/FTO exhibited a low photocurrent density of 0.75 µA cm−2 (curve a) under 420 nm visible irradiation. However, the photocurrent density of ZnPc/TiO2 NRAs/FTO increased obviously to 1.63 µA cm−2 (curve b), which was more two folds than the TiO2 NRAs/FTO. This PEC response behavior demonstrated that the combination of ZnPc with TiO2 NRAs could greatly enhanced the light absorption and the photocurrent response ability of the ZnPc/TiO2 NRAs/FTO due to the strong electronic coupling between the excited-state ZnPc and the conduction band of TiO2 [33]. The influence of the applied potential on the photocurrent response was investigated by LSV, as shown in Fig. S3. Finally, 0.0 V was selected as the applied potential.

In addition, the loading of ZnPc with different concentrations in range of 0.1–1.0 mM on the photocurrent response of the ZnPc/TiO2 NRAs/FTO were investigated, as shown in the Fig. 4B (curve a). The photocurrent density gradually increased with the increase of the ZnPc concentrations from 0.1 to 0.5 mM, and it reached the maximum when the concentration was 0.5 mM. After increasing of the ZnPc concentrations, the photocurrent density decreased again. It might be a reasonable explanation that the lower loading of ZnPc in low concentration produced a small quantity of excited electrons, resulting in relatively weak the photocurrent response, but excessive accumulation of ZnPc on TiO2 NRAs/FTO surface increased the steric hindrance and retarded electron transfer, leading to the decrease of the photocurrent response. At the same time, the dependence of the photocurrent density on different ZnPc concentrations was also carried out in the presence of 1.0 μM BPA, as shown in curve b. We can intuitively find that the photocurrent density was increased at the same concentration of ZnPc in the presence of BPA. But the variation trend of the photocurrent density was totally consistent with the absence of BPA. When the concentration of ZnPc was 0.5 mM, the photocurrent density reached to the maximum. So we considered that the optimum concentration of ZnPc was still 0.5 mM in the presence of BPA. The concentration of 0.5 mM ZnPc was optimized to obtain the best photocurrent response. And the fabricated ZnPc/TiO2 NRAs/FTO in the optimized condition was appropriate to be as a PEC sensing platform for detection of BPA.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Highly sensitive PEC sensing for BPA
ce:section.ce:para: Scheme 1 illustrated the schematic representation of the PEC sensor construction and the proposed mechanism for the PEC detection of BPA. TiO2 NRAs in-situ growth on the FTO surface was first fabricated. Next, the TiO2 NRAs/FTO surface was covered with ZnPc, using a vacuum drying method. Under 420 nm visible irradiation, ZnPc on the TiO2 NRAs surface absorbed higher energy and the electrons were excited from the HOMO to the LUMO, then the excited electrons were rapidly transferred to the conduction band of TiO2 NRAs due to its lower conduction band edge potential than ZnPc. Meanwhile, the holes would be transferred from valence band of TiO2 NRAs to ZnPc. When BPA was added, the holes would oxidize BPA, which led to the increase of photocurrent and further enhanced charge separation efficiency of photogenerated electron-holes. The increment of photocurrent could be used to detect quantitatively for BPA.

The analytical performance of the PEC sensor based on ZnPc/TiO2 NRAs/FTO for determination of BPA was investigated. Fig. 5A showed a typical photocurrent density-time curve in the presence of different concentrations of BPA. The photocurrent density increased constantly with increasing concentrations of BPA. The relationship between the photocurrent density and BPA concentrations was shown in Fig. 5B. When the concentrations of BPA were low, it could be rapidly and fully oxidized on the PEC sensing interface, leading to increasing of the photocurrent density. While the concentrations of BPA increased to 18.7 μM, the photocurrent density increased very slowly, and almost trended towards the maximum. This result could be ascribed to the fact that excessive BPA was present on the ZnPc/TiO2 NRAs/FTO surface with increasing of BPA concentrations so that there was not enough time for BPA to be fully oxidized. As a result, the resistance of the interfacial charge transfer increased and the photocurrent density reached saturation. An excellent linear relationship between the photocurrent density and the logarithm of BPA concentrations (shown in the inset of Fig. 5B) was obtained in the range from 0.047 to 52.1 μM with a correlation coefficient of 0.9919, and the regression equation was I = 0.5764 lgC + 2.4281, where I was the photocurrent density after addition of BPA, C was the BPA concentration. The confidence intervals for the slope and the intercept of the calibration curve were 0.5764 ± 0.0290 and 2.4281 ± 0.0365, respectively. The detection limit was calculated to be 8.6 nM based on 3 σ/S, in which σ is the standard deviation of a blank signal, and S is the slope of the linear calibration plot. And the value of the sensitivity is 0.5764 μA L μmol−1. Here, the analytical behavior of the present PEC sensor was compared with other analytical methods, and the results were shown in Table 1. It could be observed clearly that the detection limit of this sensor was comparable or even lower than other reported methods. More importantly, this constructed sensor was simple, fast and low cost. This proposed method could potentially be applied to determine sensitively BPA present in plastic products and food.

The reproducibility was evaluated by the detection of 1.0 μM BPA using the same sensor with five replicate measurements. The relative standard deviation (RSD) was 4.3%. Meanwhile, five same independently prepared sensors were used to determine 1.0 μM BPA, a RSD of 3.2% was obtained. These results indicated that the PEC sensor has good reproducibility.

The stability of the PEC sensor was an important factor in the practical application. The photocurrent responses were recorded as the excitation light was turned on and off every 50 s, shown in Fig. 6A. The irradiation process was repeated more than 25 times over 2500 s. It was observed obviously that the photocurrent density was very stable without any noticeable change, demonstrating the excellent stability of the PEC sensor at 420 nm visible irradiation.

In addition, the long-term stability of the prepared PEC sensor was also investigated by measuring the photocurrent density of 1.0 μM BPA. The same PEC sensor was stored at 4 °C in a refrigerator for different periods before the PEC detection. It was observed that 95% of the original response was retained when storage for one week. However, the photocurrent density still retained 80% of initial signal after 15 days under the same conditions. These results indicated that the sensor had a favorable stability and could be applied to the real samples detection.

To reveal the selectivity of BPA PEC sensor, and considering that dyes and polycyclic aromatic compounds commonly could be coexisted with BPA in the environment. The potential interferents chosen including methyl orange (MO), sudan Ⅲ, phenanthrene (Phe), anthracene (Ant) and fluoranthene (Fla), had been determined using the same method for the detection of BPA. As shown in Fig. 6B, the photocurrent density of 1.0 μM BPA and two-component system containing one of the interferents with the different concentrations and 1.0 μM BPA were measured under the optimum conditions. It could be seen that only slight difference of the photocurrent density was caused by 100-fold concentrations of MO, sudan Ⅲ, and 50-fold concentrations of Phe, Ant, Fla in the presence of 1.0 μM BPA, and all of the deviations were less than 4.1%. At the same time, the photocurrent density of all the interferents present with 1.0 μM BPA was also investigated, and the deviation was 5.6%, indicating the excellent selectivity of the PEC sensor for BPA determination.

In order to investigate the feasibility of the developed sensor in practical analytical applications, four samples including PC baby bottle、PC bottle、PVC food package and milk were chosen and the level of BPA in these real samples was evaluated. Furthermore, the recovery experiments were performed via a standard addition method and the results were shown in Table 2.

It was observed clearly that a little of BPA was present in the plastic products except for milk. This could be attributed to the fact that BPA was present in the packaging bag in the process of production of plastic products, but it did not migrate to the milk. According to GB9685-2008, the content of BPA in food containers and packaging material should be less than 0.6 mg kg−1. So the level of BPA in these chosen samples were no more than this standard, which can preliminarily demonstrate that these products on the market are in a safe range. Then the recovery tests were carried out in order to evaluate the accuracy of the method. 1.0 μM BPA was added to four real samples after each real sample was measured directly. The recoveries were in the range from 97% to 105.6% with a relative standard deviation less than 4.16% through five measurements, indicating that the present PEC method had good accuracy and could be successfully applied to evaluate the level of BPA in plastic products and food.

In this work, a sensitive and simple PEC sensing platform based on ZnPc/TiO2 NRAs/FTO was successfully prepared for detection of BPA under visible light. The vertically aligned 1D nanostructure TiO2 NRAs on FTO was first synthesized by simple one-step hydrothermal method. Subsequently, the TiO2 NRAs/FTO surface was covered with ZnPc. The combination of ZnPc with TiO2 NRAs/FTO successfully extended the absorption of TiO2 NRAs to the visible region. At the same time, the ZnPc-TiO2 NRAs composite improved greatly the charge transfer ability, which was beneficial to the separation of photogenerated electrons and holes. The developed PEC sensor based on ZnPc/TiO2 NRAs/FTO for detection of BPA exhibited excellent analytical performance with high sensitivity and selectivity. A wide linear work range from 0.047 to 52.1 μM was obtained with a low detection limit of 8.6 nM. Meanwhile, the PEC sensor possessed the advantages including rapid response, great stability and reproducibility. In addition, the PEC sensor was successfully applied to detect the level of BPA in real samples, with a satisfactory recovery ranging from 97% to 105.6%. Thus, a rapid, simple and real-time analytical method was established for evaluating the level of BPA in environment and food.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was financially supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (NSFC, Nos. 21707082, 21575084 and 21475080), the Natural Science Foundation of Shanxi Province, China (No. 201601D202008).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found in the online version at doi:10.1016/j.talanta.2018.06.043.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Bisphenol A: an endocrine disruptor with widespread exposure and multiple effects
